title: From hyperbolic billiards to statistical physics
speaker: Peter Nandori
affiliation: Yeshiva University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Consider a point particle flying freely on the torus and elastically bouncing back from the boundary of fixed smooth convex obstacles. This is the celebrated Sinai billiard, a rare example of a deterministic dynamical system where rigorous results relevant for statistical physics are available. In this talk, we will briefly review the classical theory (ergodicity, mixing, central limit theorem by Sinai, Bunimovich, Chernov, etc.).

proof_stages.label: Hard-ball ergodic hypothesis as the source problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Boltzmann's hard-ball gas, the Boltzmann--Sinai ergodic hypothesis, Sinai's torus result, and localized hard-ball models as the physical motivation for billiard dynamics.

proof_stages.label: One-particle Sinai billiards as a tractable hyperbolic model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines finite-horizon Sinai billiard flow and map, then reviews the established statistical theory: hyperbolicity, ergodicity, mixing, CLT and local CLT, exponential correlation decay, standard pairs, and recent flow-level versions.

proof_stages.label: Transport challenge and existing interacting-particle limits
proof_stages.summary: The noninteracting Lorentz gas is identified as insufficient for heat conduction, motivating interacting particles and comparing known perturbative or Boltzmann--Grad approaches that take different limits.

proof_stages.label: Non-equilibrium particle density for noninteracting Lorentz gases
proof_stages.summary: For a Lorentz tube or scaled planar domain with Poisson injection and absorbing boundaries, the expected particle density is shown to converge either to a linear steady profile or, under diffusive scaling, to the heat equation.

proof_stages.label: Rare interactions and the first-collision law
proof_stages.summary: Because full interacting hard-ball systems lead to poorly understood high-dimensional billiards, the rare-interaction regime is introduced, and for two small billiard balls the rescaled first collision time is proved to have an exponential law for almost every speed ratio.

proof_stages.label: Mesoscopic Markov-process program
proof_stages.summary: The Gaspard--Gilbert-style program is described: derive a pure jump Markov process for rare energy exchanges at mesoscopic time scales, then study the hydrodynamic limit of the resulting stochastic interacting particle system, with non-gradient difficulties.

proof_stages.label: Particle-and-piston reduction and Markov energy limit
proof_stages.summary: The current work replaces two moving particles by a particle plus a one-dimensional piston, unfolds the model to avoid corner singularities, reduces it to a controlled three-dimensional billiard, and overcomes the unstable-to-stable cone obstruction to obtain convergence of energies to a pure jump Markov process on the epsilon^{-2} time scale.

examples.name: Large hard balls in a reflecting box
examples.note: An audience question elicits the obstruction that two sufficiently large balls in a box may be unable to pass around each other, illustrating why torus models and radius assumptions matter for ergodicity.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes large balls in a reflecting box as a possible obstruction to ergodicity because they cannot pass around one another, but does not work out the invariant components or any calculation.

passes.reason: used as the large-radius obstruction to ergodicity in a reflecting box
large radii put the reflecting-box system in a blocking boundary regime, used as an obstruction to ergodicity
The balls are large enough to collapse the exchange passage in configuration space, and this is used as the obstruction to ergodicity in a reflecting box.
large balls in a reflecting box are cited as the obstruction to box ergodicity, since they may be unable to pass or swap.
Large radii put the reflecting-box hard-ball system in a high-density edge regime and are used as the obstruction to ergodicity in a box.

examples.name: Infinite-horizon Sinai billiards
examples.note: The infinite-horizon variant is mentioned to contrast the finite-horizon case used in the talk, with the main change being a logarithmic correction in the central-limit scaling.
examples.engagement_reason: Infinite-horizon Sinai billiards are only contrasted with the finite-horizon case, with the comment that a logarithmic correction appears.

passes.reason: unbounded free-flight corridors form an infinite-horizon boundary case and force a logarithmic correction to the stated finite-horizon CLT scaling

examples.name: Geodesic flow on a negatively curved surface
examples.note: The comparison with negative-curvature geodesic flow clarifies that the planar periodic-extension interpretation of displacement is special to the torus/Lorentz-gas setting, even though CLTs for observables also exist in hyperbolic systems.
examples.engagement_reason: Geodesic flow on a negatively curved surface is discussed only as an analogy/comparison for hyperbolic dynamics and CLTs for observables.

examples.name: Bunimovich stadium
examples.note: The stadium billiard is used as a contrasting nonuniformly hyperbolic billiard where correlations decay polynomially rather than exponentially, motivating the restriction to strictly convex finite-horizon Sinai billiards.
examples.engagement_reason: The Bunimovich stadium is cited as a billiard with polynomial rather than exponential decay, with no derivation of the rate.

passes.reason: used to show exponential decay fails in nonuniformly hyperbolic billiards, with only polynomial decay
the stadium’s flat parallel sides create marginal bouncing-ball behavior with sharp slow polynomial decay, contradicting exponential decay

the stadium’s flat sides create marginal bouncing-ball orbits and it is cited to refute exponential correlation decay.

examples.name: Exponential clocks in pure jump Markov processes
examples.note: The memoryless inter-jump times of a pure jump Markov process provide the heuristic reason one should expect the first rare collision time of nearly independent billiard particles to be exponentially distributed.
examples.engagement_reason: Exponential waiting times for pure jump Markov processes are invoked as a memoryless analogy for rare collisions, not computed for a specific process.

passes.reason: ordinary exponential waiting times are invoked as the memoryless Markov-process model for rare collisions
exponential waiting times are the ordinary memoryless clocks for pure jump Markov dynamics
Exponential clocks are the standard memoryless waiting times for pure jump Markov processes, used as a model for rare collisions.
exponential clocks are the standard memoryless waiting times for pure jump Markov processes.

exponential holding times are the ordinary memoryless clocks of pure-jump Markov processes
exponential waiting times are the ordinary memoryless clocks of pure jump Markov processes
exponential clocks in pure jump Markov processes serve as the standard memoryless model for waiting times.
Used only as the standard memoryless-clock analogy for rare collisions in Markov jump dynamics.
exponential waiting times are the ordinary memoryless clocks for pure jump Markov processes

examples.name: Stationary target as a hole in phase space
examples.note: The limiting case lambda equals zero, where one particle is fixed and the other escapes through a small hole around it, relates the collision theorem to known open-system or hitting-time results.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the two-particle collision formula to the stationary-target case, noting the cancellation giving rate 1/omega and interpreting it as a hole in phase space.

passes.reason: the target is the zero-speed limiting case, turning one particle into an absorbing hole
the zero-speed endpoint freezes one particle and collapses the collision problem to hitting a small phase-space hole
The lambda=0 case freezes one particle into an absorbing hole and is the endpoint of the relative-speed parameter.
the stationary target is the zero-speed endpoint where a two-particle collision problem collapses to hitting an absorbing hole.
The stationary target is the zero-speed endpoint that collapses the two-particle collision problem to hitting an absorbing hole.
setting one particle’s speed to zero collapses the two-particle problem to a boundary case of hitting an absorbing hole
A stationary target is the zero-speed endpoint that degenerates the two-particle collision problem into a hitting-time problem for a hole.
lambda equals zero freezes one particle, an endpoint reduction to an open system with a phase-space hole
freezing one particle is the zero-speed endpoint and collapses the interaction problem to a fixed absorbing hole
a stationary target corresponds to the boundary case of zero speed and reduces the two-particle collision problem to a small hole in phase space.
The lambda=0 endpoint collapses one moving particle to a fixed absorbing hole, serving as a known limiting case.
the lambda=0 stationary-particle endpoint reduces the collision problem to hitting an absorbing hole

examples.name: Marklof--Strömbergsson Boltzmann--Grad limit
examples.note: The Q&A comparison with the periodic Lorentz gas in the Boltzmann--Grad limit illustrates a different scaling regime, using homogeneous dynamics, that is not bridged by the rare-interaction results of the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: The Marklof--Strömbergsson Boltzmann--Grad limit is cited and briefly characterized as a different limiting regime, without running through the construction.

passes.reason: the Boltzmann--Grad regime is a singular dilute limit with scatterer radius tending to zero
the Boltzmann--Grad regime is a zero-radius critical scaling limit cited as a different approach rather than a refutation

examples.name: Long flights in high-dimensional infinite-horizon billiards
examples.note: The speaker describes a related side problem in which tangent planes to spherical scatterers produce tiny effective scatterers, serving as a possible geometric comparison between fixed-scatterer and Boltzmann--Grad regimes.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker geometrically analyzes long flights in high-dimensional infinite-horizon billiards via tangent hyperplanes, tiny scatterer intersections, and applying the Boltzmann--Grad picture to first exits from corridors.
